detail: criteria.7.notes: Too big: expected string to have <=800 characters
raw: {"candidateId":"kl-divergence-v0-001","subjectId":"kl-divergence","promptVariantId":"whiteboard-v0","criteria":[{"id":"payoff-concreteness","label":"Payoff concreteness","max":8,"score":6,"notes":"The payoff names the concrete behavioral difference (mean-seeking vs mode-seeking, covering all modes versus collapsing onto one) with a specific bimodal consequence. It lacks a number or quantitative stake but is specific enough to be actionable. Scores in the upper-generic-to-low-concrete band."},{"id":"staircase-coherence","label":"Staircase coherence","max":10,"score":9,"notes":"The seven rungs build cleanly: definition, formula, forward-KL intuition, reverse-KL intuition, mass-covering consequence, mode-seeking consequence, practical rule. Each rung is necessary and the sequence is monotonic. Minor deduction: rungs 3 and 4 (the two zero-avoidance intuitions) could be considered slightly out of order relative to how the narration presents them (forward shown before both are introduced), but this is minimal."},{"id":"destination-precision","label":"Destination precision","max":8,"score":8,"notes":"The destination names three precise deliverables: hold the KL formula in both directions, explain asymmetry via the log ratio weighting, and predict mean-seeking versus mode-seeking from the direction chosen. All are concrete and testable outcomes."},{"id":"narration-clarity","label":"Narration clarity","max":12,"score":10,"notes":"P and Q are defined before the formula appears. The log ratio surprise penalty is explained immediately after the formula. 'Mean-seeking' and 'mode-seeking' are named and immediately illustrated. 'Variational inference' and 'VAE' appear late in the narration (at second 204 beat) as application labels without unpacking what those algorithms are -- acceptable at intermediate level but a minor clarity loss. 'MLE' is similarly unexpanded. No undefined symbols or contradictions otherwise."},{"id":"board-specificity","label":"Board specificity","max":10,"score":9,"notes":"Both draw actions are highly specific: the bimodal_P draw names axis labels, hump positions (x=2, x=6), heights (0.5), trough position (x=4), and curve label. The forward_Q draw names center (x=4), standard deviation (2.5), peak height (0.25), color (blue), and annotation text. The reverse_Q draw similarly names center (x=2), std (0.6), peak height (0.7), color (red). Write actions include actual formula content. One minor issue: the highlight actions at t=30 and t=62 reference the targetId as content rather than specifying which part to highlight, which is slightly ambiguous for a renderer."},{"id":"board-coherence","label":"Board coherence","max":10,"score":9,"notes":"The timeline is sound: labels appear before formulas, formulas before highlights, bimodal diagram before Q overlays, Q overlays before their labels, applications before summary. The erase at t=240 removes asymmetry_note to make room for summary_rule in the same region -- sensible and clean. Minor: by t=204 the board has formulas (upper-center, center), asymmetry note (right-center), bimodal diagrams (lower-left, lower-right), and labels -- potentially dense but not impossibly cluttered given a full whiteboard canvas."},{"id":"board-visual-balance","label":"Board visual balance","max":8,"score":8,"notes":"Three distinct draw actions (bimodal_P, forward_Q overlay, reverse_Q on mirrored axes) plus multiple writes and highlights. The visual-to-text ratio is appropriate for a probability distribution topic. Diagram and text are well-distributed across the canvas regions."},{"id":"math-notation-correctness","label":"Math and notation correctness","max":10,"score":9,"notes":"KL(P||Q) = sum_x P(x) log(P(x)/Q(x)) is correct. KL(Q||P) = sum_x Q(x) log(Q(x)/P(x)) is correct. The zero-avoidance arguments are mathematically valid: log(P/0) -> infinity when Q=0 and P>0 forces Q to cover P; log(Q/0) -> infinity when P=0 and Q>0 repels Q from zero-P regions. Narration correctly states expectation is taken under P for forward KL and under Q for reverse KL. Minor: the narration says 'log of Q over zero blows up' for the reverse KL case but the correct zero-avoidance argument for reverse KL is that Q is repelled from regions where P=0 (not Q=0), because log(Q/P) diverges when P->0 and Q>0; the statement 'if Q wanders into a region where P of x equals zero' is correct, but the phrasing 'log of Q over zero blows up' correctly identifies the mechanism. Notation is consistent throughout."},{"id":"code-content-decision","label":"Code-content decision","max":6,"score":6,"notes":"No code is included. For a probability-stats conceptual lesson at intermediate level focused on geometric intuition and formula derivation, omitting code is the correct decision. Code would distract from the mathematical mechanism being taught. The decision is correct and well-executed."},{"id":"pacing-and-density","label":"Pacing and density","max":10,"score":8,"notes":"12 beats over 270 seconds averages about 22 seconds per beat, appropriate for intermediate material. The longest gap is beat at t=90 (forward KL zero-avoidance) spanning ~40 seconds of narration text covering two staircase rungs -- slightly dense but the text is long enough to justify it. Beat at t=204 covers applications for both forward and reverse KL in one beat (~36 seconds) which is moderately loaded. Total duration of 270 seconds (4.5 minutes) is reasonable for the scope. Final beat at t=240 leaves 30 seconds to end, which is fine for a summary rule."},{"id":"redundancy-and-focus","label":"Redundancy and focus","max":8,"score":7,"notes":"The lesson stays tightly on the question. The bimodal example is introduced once and reused efficiently for both forward and reverse KL. The summary rule at t=240 echoes the payoff but adds value as a mnemonic closing. Minor: the applications beat (t=204) and the summary rule (t=240) both restate the forward/reverse distinction, creating slight overlap, though the summary is terse enough to serve as a closer rather than a true repetition."}],"totalScore":89,"maxScore":100,"percent":89,"topThreeIssues":["The narration introduces 'variational inference', 'VAE', and 'MLE' at the t=204 beat without any unpacking -- at intermediate level these are high-leverage application anchors and even a one-clause gloss ('MLE, the standard way of fitting models to data') would sharpen clarity without adding length.","The t=90 and t=204 beats each carry two staircase rungs simultaneously; splitting the forward-KL zero-avoidance explanation (currently covering both the infinity argument and the mass-covering consequence) into two beats with a board action between them would reduce cognitive load at the densest moment.","The highlight board actions at t=30 and t=62 specify only the targetId as content rather than which portion of the formula to highlight (e.g., the log-ratio term, or the outer weighting factor), leaving a renderer without enough information to produce a precise highlight -- replace content with a specific sub-expression or a region description."],"wouldRecommendForPipeline":true,"summary":"This is a strong lesson plan that correctly answers the question, builds a coherent staircase, and uses a concrete bimodal example to make asymmetry visually obvious. The board actions are unusually specific for draw operations and the math is correct throughout. The main improvement opportunities are unpacking the three algorithm names introduced at the end, splitting the densest beat, and making highlight actions more precise."}
